retrieved_docs.title: Tetracycline -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracyclines are contraindicated in pregnancy because of the risk of hepatotoxicity in the mother, the potential for permanent discoloration of teeth in the fetus (yellow or brown in appearance), as well as impairment of fetal long bone growth. Tetracycline usage is also associated with teeth discoloration in children under the age of eight. Thus it should be avoided in pediatric patients under that age.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: To prevent tooth discoloration, pediatric patients younger than 8 years of age co-infected with Borrelia burgdorferi should remain on doxycycline until they have been afebrile for 3 days. They should then switch to an active agent against the pathogen, for example, amoxicillin or cefuroxime, for the remainder of the 14-day course. Of note, if a patient does not show signs of clinical improvement after 3 days of doxycycline therapy, the patient should be evaluated and treated for possible Babesia infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Acne Vulgaris -- Treatment / Management -- Oral Antibiotics
retrieved_docs.content: Oral antibiotics effectively inhibit the growth of C acnes within the pilosebaceous unit. Tetracyclines, in particular, have anti-inflammatory properties, making them the preferred antibiotic for treating acne vulgaris. If tetracyclines are ineffective or not well-tolerated by patients, other antibiotics (such as macrolides, cephalosporins, penicillins, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) may be considered alternatives. [41] To reduce the development of antibiotic resistance, it is necessary to limit the duration of the treatment to the shortest possible period (ideally, 3-4 months). This can be complemented by the concurrent use of a topical retinoid, which can aid in the discontinuation of the antibiotic, or by incorporating the use of benzoyl peroxide. Preferred tetracyclines for acne treatment include doxycycline, minocycline, and sarecycline. Doxycycline and other tetracyclines should not be prescribed to patients who are children aged less than 8 or pregnant women. Long-term treatment with oral antibiotics may lead to tooth discoloration in developing teeth. Moreover, doxycycline can cause gastrointestinal distress, esophagitis, and pseudotumor cerebri (idiopathic intracranial hypertension). Photosensitivity is also one of the adverse effects of oral antibiotics; therefore, sun protective measures are advised while on treatment with tetracyclines.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Antibiotics
retrieved_docs.content: It is advisable to prescribe the patient doxycycline for five days or amoxicillin in case of intolerance. In children under 50 kg, the regime includes an initial dose of 100 mg doxycycline on the first day and 50 mg on the four consecutive days. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (Rickettsia rickettsii) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Doxycycline is the drug of choice for the treatment of Rocky Mountain spotted fever, including in children. Defervescence typically occurs within three days of starting appropriate antibiotic therapy, although other symptoms may be slower to resolve. The treatment course is usually seven to ten days, or for at least three days after defervescence. It is important to note than in severe rickettsial disease, such as Rocky Mountain spotted fever, mortality rates are as high as 20% to 30% without prompt antibiotic treatment. Thus, treatment should not delay while awaiting confirmatory laboratory testing in a patient with a suspected rickettsial infection; this is particularly important in children, who are at high risk of morbidity and mortality but may experience delays in treatment due to concerns with doxycycline use in children younger than age 8. The risk of dental staining in the treatment of Rocky Mountain spotted fever is very low, as dental staining is a cumulative dose effect, and treatment for Rocky Mountain spotted fever is typically seven to ten days. Thus, concerns regarding tooth staining should not delay treatment in suspected Rocky Mountain spotted fever. [11] [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Lyme Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Special Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Doxycycline is used in most patients except in young children and those who are pregnant. In children, amoxicillin remains the drug of choice. For patients older than 8 with early, localized disease, doxycycline is recommended for 10 days. Patients younger than 8 should receive amoxicillin or cefuroxime for 14 days to avoid the potential for tooth staining caused by tetracycline use in young children. [19] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Anthrax Infection -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Doxycycline (tetracycline): Associated with photosensitivity, esophageal irritation, and tooth discoloration in children younger than 8 years; however, its benefits outweigh risks in the context of anthrax

retrieved_docs.title: Doxycycline Hyclate -- Administration -- Breastfeeding
retrieved_docs.content: Although product labeling states that tetracyclines, including doxycycline, are excreted in human milk and that the extent of infant absorption remains uncertain, available evidence suggests that short-term maternal use may carry minimal risk due to low drug concentrations in milk and reduced oral absorption in the infant due to calcium binding. However, given the theoretical risks of dental discoloration, effects on bone growth, and alteration of the infant's gastrointestinal flora, prolonged or repeated courses should be avoided during breastfeeding unless absolutely necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, research shows that if teeth are exposed to tetracycline at the time of tooth calcification and mineralization, tetracycline binds calcium ions in the teeth. If this occurs before tooth eruption, the bound tetracycline causes the teeth to erupt with an initial fluorescent yellow discoloration. Once the teeth have been exposed to light for a few months to years, tetracycline oxidizes, and the discoloration changes to a nonfluorescent brown or gray. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Minocycline -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: Minocycline is classified by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as a pregnancy Category D medicine. Due to their potential for permanent discoloration of teeth and impairment of long bone growth in the fetus, pregnant women should avoid tetracyclines. [16] Notably, the use of topical minocycline products is not contraindicated during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Minocycline -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: As all tetracyclines, including minocycline, can cross the placental barrier, they are contraindicated during pregnancy. The additional risk associated with hepatotoxicity in pregnant individuals is also a factor that underscores the importance of avoiding the use of this drug in such cases. [33] [34] Individuals aged 8 or younger should avoid using minocycline to prevent permanent teeth discoloration for teeth that have not yet erupted. Therefore, pediatric patients between the ages of 13 and 19 should avoid minocycline use until all of their tooth crowns have developed. [35] Clinicians should be aware that the administration of expired tetracyclines can lead to the development of Fanconi syndrome. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Doxycycline Hyclate -- Adverse Effects -- Common Adverse Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: The most common adverse reactions associated with doxycycline include: Mild diarrhea Photosensitivity Nausea Vomiting Skin rash/itching Headaches Tooth discoloration Fixed drug eruption [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Botulin Toxin A Injection

retrieved_docs.title: Ophthalmia Neonatorum -- Toxicokinetics -- Toxicity Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic toxicity from ocular prophylaxis is exceedingly rare. In theory, absorbed erythromycin could lead to gastrointestinal upset or contribute to hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, but epidemiologic data have not demonstrated a causal relationship at ophthalmic dosing. Tetracycline ointment theoretically raises concerns for tooth discoloration or bone deposition if systemically absorbed over prolonged periods; however, short-term use in neonates does not result in clinically significant exposure. In summary, the toxicokinetics of ophthalmia neonatorum prophylaxis are characterized by predominantly local drug action with minimal systemic absorption, limited metabolism due to the small amount of absorbed dose, and rapid local elimination via tear flow and nasolacrimal drainage. Understanding these processes ensures that agents chosen for neonatal eye care maximize antimicrobial efficacy while minimizing the risk of local irritation or systemic toxicity. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsing Fever -- Treatment / Management -- Relapsing Fever Management
retrieved_docs.content: Relapsing fever is treated with doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 to 10 days. Doxycycline acts on the 30S ribosomal subunit to inhibit bacterial protein synthesis, resulting in bacteriostatic activity. In pregnant women and children younger than 8 years of age, penicillin or erythromycin are the preferred agents due to the concern of dental staining with doxycycline use, despite tooth staining being associated with doxycycline cumulative dose rather than as an idiosyncratic reaction. No discernible difference is observed in clinical outcomes in patients treated with >10 days of doxycycline compared with <10 days. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Nerve Palsy -- Treatment / Management -- Medical
retrieved_docs.content: Lyme disease management depends on patient age and disease severity. If localized disease in an individual over the age of eight, 200 mg once daily doxycycline for a total of 10 days is recommended. If under the age of eight, a 14-day course of amoxicillin or cefuroxime to avoid potential tooth staining with tetracycline use. [20] For most early and localized cases, treatment usually is curative.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 481
retrieved_docs.content: Minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, and tigecycline are all members of the tetracycline antibiotic class. Various formulations of these agents are available for clinical use, including 24‑hour extended‑release oral capsules of minocycline (135 mg and 45 mg), immediate‑release oral capsules and tablets of minocycline (50 mg and 75 mg), oral capsules of doxycycline hyclate (100 mg) and doxycycline monohydrate (50 mg and 100 mg), delayed‑release oral tablets of doxycycline hyclate (50 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, and 200 mg), and injectable preparations of doxycycline hyclate (100 mg) and tigecycline (50 mg). Because they share the tetracycline antibiotic classification, these drugs exert a common mechanism of action—binding to the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit to inhibit protein synthesis—and therefore have overlapping antimicrobial spectra and similar safety considerations, such as photosensitivity and potential effects on bone and teeth development. Recognizing that all listed dosage forms belong to the same antibiotic family aids clinicians in selecting appropriate formulations for specific infections, routes of administration, and patient needs while monitoring for class‑related adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: Minocycline 50 MG Oral Capsule, 24 HR Minocycline 45 MG Extended‑Release Oral Tablet, doxycycline hyclate 50 MG Oral Capsule, doxycycline hyclate 50 MG Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet, doxycycline hyclate 100 MG Oral Capsule, doxycycline hyclate 100 MG Injection, doxycycline hyclate 150 MG Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet, doxycycline hyclate 200 MG Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet, doxycycline monohydrate 75 MG Oral Capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 100 MG Oral Capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 150 MG Oral Capsule, tetracycline hydrochloride 250 MG Oral Capsule, and tigecycline 50 MG Injection are all classified as tetracycline antibiotics. These agents share a common mechanism of action in which they bind to the bacterial 30 S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis and thereby exerting broad‑spectrum antibacterial activity. The class includes a variety of dosage forms—oral capsules, extended‑release and delayed‑release tablets, and injectable preparations—allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the infection site, severity, and patient compliance needs. While most of the listed drugs are traditional tetracyclines (minocycline, doxycycline, tetracycline hydrochloride), tigecycline represents a glycylcycline derivative that retains the core tetracycline structure but offers activity against many tetracycline‑resistant organisms. Understanding that these diverse formulations all belong to the tetracycline antibiotic class helps guide appropriate selection, dosing strategies, and monitoring for class‑related adverse effects such as photosensitivity, gastrointestinal upset, and potential effects on bone and tooth development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1469
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracycline and its derivatives formulated for topical application are classified as antibiotics for topical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1583
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the tetracycline group, indicating that these locally applied oral antimicrobial agents belong to the broader tetracycline class of antibiotics and share its characteristic antimicrobial properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge highlights a broad spectrum of oral and injectable tetracycline antibiotics and related agents, emphasizing their dosage forms, strengths, and shared excipients. Minocycline is available in immediate‑release capsules and tablets (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg) and in extended‑release capsules and tablets (24 HR 45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg), all classified as tetracycline antibiotics; the 75 mg tablet and the tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg and 500 mg capsules also contain the coloring excipient oxid železnato‑železitý (iron oxide). Demeclocycline hydrochloride tablets (150 mg, 300 mg) are tetracycline antibiotics that use alginic acid as a stabilizing excipient. Doxycycline is marketed in two salt forms: hyclate (50 mg and 100 mg oral capsules, 100 mg injection, delayed‑release tablets 50 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg) and monohydrate (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg oral capsules). All doxycycline products belong to the tetracycline class. Additional tetracycline agents include tetracycline hydrochloride oral capsules (250 mg, 500 mg) and tigecycline injection (50 mg). Beyond tetracyclines, galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg) and levofloxacin oral tablets (500 mg, 750 mg) also incorporate oxid železnato‑železitý, linking these non‑tetracycline drugs to the same iron‑oxide coloring agent. Awareness of the common presence of oxid železnato‑železitý across multiple antibiotics and the cholinesterase inhibitor, as well as the use of alginic acid in demeclocycline, is important for clinicians when assessing patient sensitivities, contraindications, and selecting appropriate formulations to optimize therapeutic outcomes and tolerability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment is a therapeutic category that includes doxycycline and minocyklin. Both doxycycline and minocyklin are members of this class, indicating they are used as local oral anti‑infective and antiseptic agents. Their inclusion in the same category suggests they share similar indications for treating oral infections and can be considered when selecting topical antimicrobial therapy for dental or oral conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 826
retrieved_docs.content: Minocycline 45 mg extended‑release oral capsule, Minocycline 45 mg extended‑release oral tablet, Minocycline 90 mg extended‑release oral capsule, Minocycline 100 mg oral tablet, and Minocycline 50 mg oral tablet are all classified as tetracycline antibiotics. Doxycycline hyclate 100 mg injection, Doxycycline hyclate 150 mg delayed‑release oral tablet, Doxycycline hyclate 200 mg delayed‑release oral tablet, Doxycycline hyclate 50 mg delayed‑release oral tablet, Doxycycline monohydrate 100 mg oral capsule, and Doxycycline monohydrate 75 mg oral capsule are likewise members of the tetracycline antibiotic class. Tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg oral capsule and Tigecycline 50 mg injection are additional agents that belong to the same tetracycline antibiotic family. All of these formulations share the core pharmacologic properties of tetracycline antibiotics, including inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30S ribosomal subunit, a broad spectrum of activity against gram‑positive and gram‑negative organisms, and common adverse effect profiles such as photosensitivity, gastrointestinal upset, and potential effects on bone and tooth development. Recognizing that each product represents a specific dosage form or release characteristic of the underlying tetracycline class helps clinicians select the appropriate agent for infections where tetracyclines are indicated, while also monitoring for class‑related toxicities and drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 377
retrieved_docs.content: Minocycline, doxycycline, tetracycline hydrochloride, and tigecycline are all classified as tetracycline antibiotics, a broad-spectrum antimicrobial class used to treat a variety of bacterial infections. Minocycline is available in several oral dosage forms, including a 45 mg extended‑release capsule and tablet, a 50 mg oral capsule and tablet, and a 75 mg oral tablet, each of which belongs to the tetracycline antibiotic family. Doxycycline is marketed in multiple formulations: a 100 mg injection, a 100 mg oral capsule, delayed‑release tablets in 50 mg, 150 mg, and 200 mg strengths, and a 150 mg oral capsule of doxycycline monohydrate, all of which are tetracycline antibiotics. Additionally, tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg oral capsule and tigecycline 50 mg injection are also members of the tetracycline antibiotic class. Recognizing that these diverse dosage forms and strengths share a common pharmacologic class is essential for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy, managing potential class‑related adverse effects, and ensuring consistent antimicrobial stewardship across different patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid železnato‑železitý, an iron‑based excipient, is incorporated in a wide range of oral dosage forms, including the 24‑hour extended‑release capsules of galantamine hydrobromide (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg) and numerous tetracycline‑class antibiotics such as all extended‑release minocycline capsules (45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg), minocycline tablets and capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg), doxycycline hyclate capsules (50 mg, 100 mg) and delayed‑release tablets (50 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg), doxycycline monohydrate capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), tetracycline hydrochloride capsules (250 mg, 500 mg), and the tetracycline‑hydrochloride 500 mg capsule. The repeated use of this iron‑derived compound suggests it provides matrix stability, binding, or flow properties, and clinicians should consider possible sensitivities or contraindications to iron compounds when prescribing these products. Tetracycline antibiotics—including minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, tetracycline

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the tetracyklin group, and tetracyklin is identified as a subcategory within the broader class of Tetracycline Antibiotics. This hierarchy indicates that the oral anti‑infective preparations belonging to tetracyklin share the characteristic antimicrobial properties of tetracycline antibiotics, while also being specifically formulated for local use in the mouth. Recognizing that tetracyklin agents are part of the larger Tetracycline Antibiotics family helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action, spectrum of activity, and appropriate therapeutic role in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracycline and its derivatives formulated for topical use are classified as members of the tetracycline drug class and are identified as a type of antibiotic specifically intended for topical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: sensitivities or contraindications to iron compounds when prescribing these products. Tetracycline antibiotics—including minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, tetracycline hydrochloride, demeclocycline hydrochloride (150 mg, 300 mg), and tigecycline (50 mg injection)—share a common mechanism of action: inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30S ribosomal subunit. This confers broad‑spectrum activity against gram‑positive and gram‑negative bacteria and atypical pathogens such as Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, and Rickettsia. Formulations range from immediate‑release capsules to extended‑release capsules, delayed‑release tablets, and parenteral injections, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to infection site, patient tolerance, and dosing convenience. Class‑wide considerations include the risk of resistance, contraindications in pregnancy and early childhood, and adverse effects such as photosensitivity, gastrointestinal upset, and, for minocycline,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid železnato‑železitý (iron oxide) is a shared inactive ingredient that appears in a wide array of oral capsule and tablet products, including the 24‑hour extended‑release galantamine hydrobromide capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg), all listed 24‑hour minocycline extended‑release forms (45 mg capsule, 45 mg tablet, 90 mg capsule, 135 mg capsule), multiple doxycycline hyclate capsules (50 mg, 100 mg) and delayed‑release tablets (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg), doxycycline monohydrate capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), immediate‑release minocycline capsules and tablets (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg), and tetracycline hydrochloride capsules (250 mg and 500 mg). The presence of this coloring agent is clinically relevant for patients with sensitivities or allergies to iron oxide. All of the listed minocycline, doxycycline, tetracycline, demeclocycline, and tigecycline products belong to the tetracycline‑class antibiotics, which share the mechanism of inhibiting bacterial protein

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid železnato‑železitý is a widely used inactive excipient that appears in numerous oral capsule and tablet products, including 24‑hour galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg), 24‑hour minocycline extended‑release capsules (45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg), standard‑release minocycline capsules and tablets (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg), doxycycline hyclate capsules (50 mg, 100 mg) and injection (100 mg), doxycycline hyclate delayed‑release tablets (50 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg), doxycycline monohydrate capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), and tetracycline hydrochloride capsules (250 mg, 500 mg). All of the listed minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, tetracycline hydrochloride, and tigecycline (50 mg injection) products belong to the tetracycline antibiotic class, providing broad‑spectrum antibacterial activity for a variety of bacterial infections. These agents differ in dosage strength, release profile (immediate, delayed, or extended release), and route of administration (oral capsule, oral tablet, or injection), allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to infection severity and patient‑specific factors. Awareness of the shared pharmacologic class aids in selecting appropriate tetracycline therapy, optimizing dosing regimens, and recognizing potential excipient‑related sensitivities or adverse reactions associated with oxid železnato‑železitý.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the tetracycline group (tetracyklin), indicating that they belong to the broader class of tetracycline‑based agents. Likewise, tetracycline and its derivatives used as topical antibiotics are also members of the tetracycline group, and they are specifically identified as antibiotics for topical use. This demonstrates that the tetracycline class encompasses both oral anti‑infective preparations and topical antibiotic formulations, reflecting the versatility of tetracycline‑derived compounds in treating localized infections in different anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 668
retrieved_docs.content: The medications 24 HR minocycline 45 mg Extended‑Release Oral Capsule, 24 HR minocycline 90 mg Extended‑Release Oral Capsule, minocycline 100 mg Oral Capsule, and minocycline 75 mg Oral Capsule are all classified as tetracycline antibiotics. Likewise, doxycycline hyclate formulations—including the 100 mg Injection, the 100 mg Oral Capsule, the 150 mg Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet, the 200 mg Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet, and the 50 mg Delayed‑Release Oral Tablet—are tetracycline antibiotics, as are doxycycline monohydrate 150 mg Oral Capsule and doxycycline monohydrate 50 mg Oral Capsule. Additional agents within the same class are tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Capsule and tigecycline 50 mg Injection. Collectively, these agents share the pharmacologic properties of the tetracycline antibiotic family, which inhibit bacterial protein synthesis and are employed to treat a variety of susceptible infections. Recognizing that each of these specific dosage forms and strengths belongs to the tetracycline class aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related adverse effects, and managing drug interactions across the diverse formulations.
